supporting_evidence: Convergent VTE risk factors create multiplicative risk exceeding 30× baseline: drospirenone OCP (OR 6.3) × prolonged immobilization (OR 2.3) × family history thrombophilia
Prior ED visit 3 weeks ago for RIGHT CALF PAIN diagnosed as muscle strain WITHOUT Doppler — this was the DVT that has now embolized
Pleuritic chest pain + low-grade fever = classic pulmonary infarction presentation
SpO2 95% represents 3-5% drop from expected baseline of 98-100% in a healthy 28-year-old — significant A-a gradient widening
HR 102 is compensatory tachycardia from V/Q mismatch and increased dead-space ventilation
Pain reproducible on palpation does NOT exclude PE (19.9% vs 23.8%, p=0.25 — no discriminative value)

candidates.diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis — right lower extremity, propagated
supporting_evidence: Prior right calf pain 3 weeks ago was almost certainly a DVT — timing, location, and risk factor profile are all consistent
No Doppler was performed at the prior ED visit — the DVT was missed
Propagation from calf veins to proximal veins (popliteal, femoral, iliac) is expected over 3 weeks without anticoagulation
Current presentation represents embolization from this propagated DVT
Lower extremity Doppler is essential to confirm residual thrombus burden and guide anticoagulation duration
Low-grade fever 37.2°C in the context of PE suggests pulmonary infarction — occurs in 10-30% of PE cases
Pleuritic pain localized to a specific area is characteristic of wedge-shaped infarction
Peripheral emboli are more likely to cause infarction than central emboli
May develop into a pulmonary abscess if not treated — rare but serious complication

candidates.diagnosis: Musculoskeletal chest wall pain — coincidental overlay
supporting_evidence: Physical exertion 2 days ago provides a plausible mechanical trigger
Chest wall tenderness is present but has no discriminative value for excluding PE
May coexist with PE — the two diagnoses are not mutually exclusive
Must not allow this cognitive anchor to prevent PE investigation

candidates.diagnosis: Inherited thrombophilia — Factor V Leiden or prothrombin gene mutation
supporting_evidence: Family history of DVT in paternal aunt at age 45 suggests autosomal dominant thrombophilia
Factor V Leiden prevalence is 5% in the Swedish population — highest in Northern Europe
Drospirenone OCP + Factor V Leiden = synergistic VTE risk (OR 28.5 in some studies)
Thrombophilia testing should be performed AFTER acute treatment (results do not change acute management but guide lifelong anticoagulation decisions)
If confirmed, first-degree relatives should be offered testing

reasoning: This case represents a textbook example of anchoring bias leading to a missed PE diagnosis. The patient's young age, athletic appearance, clear mechanical trigger, and reproducible chest wall tenderness all conspire to anchor the clinician toward musculoskeletal pain. However, the EHR reveals a CONVERGENT pattern of VTE risk factors that should override this anchor: (1) drospirenone OCP for 14 months (highest VTE risk class, OR 6.3), (2) 12-hour car drive 5 days ago (prolonged immobilization), (3) family DVT history suggesting inherited thrombophilia, and most critically (4) a prior ED visit for right calf pain 3 weeks ago that was dismissed as muscle strain WITHOUT Doppler imaging. This prior visit was almost certainly the index DVT event. The current presentation — pleuritic chest pain, mild hypoxemia, compensatory tachycardia, low-grade fever — represents embolization from this untreated DVT. The multiplicative VTE risk exceeds 30× baseline. Modified Wells score places this patient firmly in the 'PE likely' category. Direct CTPA is indicated; D-dimer should not be used as a rule-out tool when clinical probability is this high.
